title: Table: The table shows the frequency of adults using the internet in one European count...
description: The table shows the frequency of adults using the internet in one European country from 2006 to 2010. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
guide_html:
HƯỚNG DẪN VIẾT BÀI

Frequency of activity (2006–2010)

1. Giải thích đề

Bảng cho thấy tỷ lệ (%) người tham gia một hoạt động theo tần suất (daily, weekly, monthly, never) từ 2006–2010.

→ Dạng: time series \+ categories

2. Introduction (paraphrase)

  • Giữ: percentage – frequency – over a 5-year period
  • Dùng:
  • shows → illustrates / presents
  • from 2006 to 2010 → over the period from 2006 to 2010

3. Overview (ý chính)

  • Daily tăng mạnh và trở thành cao nhất
  • Never giảm cực mạnh
  • Các nhóm còn lại biến động nhẹ / giảm dần

4. Chia BODY

BODY 1 – Xu hướng chính (Daily & Never)
  • Daily: tăng liên tục (36% → 72%)
  • Never: giảm mạnh (34% → 1%)

→ Ý: shift rõ rệt từ không tham gia → tham gia thường xuyên

BODY 2 – Nhóm còn lại
  • Weekly: tăng rồi giảm (15 → 29 → 22)
  • Monthly: giảm đều (15 → 5)

→ Ý: tần suất trung bình giảm hoặc dao động nhẹ

5. Gợi ý từ vựng

  • rise sharply → tăng mạnh
  • fall dramatically → giảm mạnh
  • fluctuate → dao động
  • steadily → đều đặn
  • overtake → vượt

Ví dụ:

  • Daily participation rose sharply over the period.
  • The proportion of people who never participated fell dramatically.
  • Weekly figures fluctuated before declining.

The table compares the frequency of an activity, measured as percentages, between 2006 and 2010.

Overall, daily participation increased markedly over the period, while the proportion of people who never engaged in the activity declined sharply. Less frequent participation, such as three times per month, also decreased, whereas weekly figures fluctuated slightly.

In 2006, daily participation stood at 36%, rising steadily to 40% in 2007 and 45% in 2008, before increasing sharply to 65% in 2009 and peaking at 72% in 2010. By contrast, the percentage of people who never took part fell dramatically from 34% in 2006 to 20% in 2007 and 16% in 2008, before dropping to just 2% in 2009 and 1% in the final year.

Weekly participation showed a more variable trend, increasing from 15% in 2006 to a peak of 29% in 2008, then declining to 25% in 2009 and 22% in 2010. Meanwhile, participation at a rate of three times per month decreased consistently from 15% in 2006 and 2007 to 10% in 2008, followed by further declines to 8% and 5% in the final two years.
